Transcrição Completa: 4 Jeremy Moser - Transformando Pessoas Desconhecidas em Marcas Pessoais de 8 Dígitos
[00:00] Thank you, Jeremy, for making it inadmissible to share this video with any client of mine. So, again, as the title says, we're going to talk about how we're going to turn no-name people into 8 figure personal brands.
[00:15] Um, just a quick overview, there's three things I'm going to cover. Um, which is first of all, just our 100 million content acquisition system. Um, second of all, sort of this overlooked tool that we use to not only get 80% plus show rates for clients time and time again, but also how thanks to Josh Troy, my new favorite metric, uh, how we get up to $2,400 plus dollars per booked call.
[00:45] And lastly, going to go over the three-part sort of copy paste blueprint that if you solely rely on paid ads, you can actually leverage to increase your ROAS thanks to content.
[01:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Who I Am - Co-founder of Social Profit Media"
NÚMEROS: 5+ anos, $100 milhões em vendas, 6 clientes com $1M/mês consistente, 13 clientes com +$500K/mês extra
VISUAL: Introdução profissional com estatísticas de resultados, layout de apresentação corporativa
[01:15] So, just a quick overview of who I am. I'm the co-founder of Social Profit Media. Me and my business partner, Yan, who's sadly not here, we run Instagram marketing agency. We've done it for over five years. Um, and through shoutouts and organic content have generated well over 100 million sales.
[01:35] Um, off of that had six clients hit consistently a million a month and 13 clients added an extra 500K per month.
[01:45] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Client Results Overview"
NÚMEROS: 8-9 clientes, $3.1M em vendas no último mês, <$30 custo por call qualificada
VISUAL: Dashboard de métricas, gráficos de performance, comparação de resultados entre clientes
[02:00] Now, with that said, I just kind of want to give you guys an overview of some of our clients are. You may know some of them or you may not know if some of them, which is probably the whole point of this presentation. And so, I just want to give you some concepts. So last month we did across 8 to 9 clients we did 3.1 million in sales.
[02:20] I think the coolest the coolest part about it is when you look at the cost per call section um we're sitting at like below $30 right now which is mostly qualified calls. So that's pretty cool.
[02:35] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Content Framework - The 80/20"
NÚMEROS: 30-50%+ engagement em posts virais
VISUAL: Fluxograma mostrando: Conteúdo → Visitas ao Perfil → Posts Virais → Theme Pages → Mais Visitas → Webinar/VSSL/DMs → Sales Call
[02:45] And the framework we use is actually pretty stupid simple. So at the core of it which is like really our 80/20 is like the content here. Um, and the content generally, if you notice, it obviously generates profile visits or like inbound leads.
[03:00] But really what we do from there is we look for like the viral content or like the outlier post, right? So they're posts that are getting like 30 50% plus engagement and just really stand out, you know, in terms of they get a bunch of qualified DMs and a bunch of, you know, new followers or, you know, you name it.
[03:20] And then from there, we basically just take those posts and we throw them on like theme pages. So, you know, if we're in the entrepreneurship space, it might be something like entrepreneurship facts or, you know, uh the business hacks, what you name it.
[03:35] So, we just pay those pages and they simply put redistribute the content and just gives us a little shout out in terms of, hey, follow this page. They've done x, y, and z.
[03:48] And from there, we just managed to get obviously number one, a ton more profile visits. And from there, either they just engage with the content, or they go directly, click the link in bio, which is simply put a webinar or a VSSL, or they go directly into the inbound DMs, and then from there, we just kind of nurture them up and push them into a sales call where hopefully a capable sales team is converting them.
[04:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "3 Crucial Components"
NÚMEROS: 3 pilares principais
VISUAL: Três colunas destacadas: 1. Content, 2. DMs, 3. Shoutouts
[04:20] Proves to be pretty hard. Um, now there's three crucial components to that whole strategy, which is one content. I want to hammer this home because like the death of my existence is when clients hit me up, they're like, "Can we get more leads?" And it's like, "Yeah, but we need good content for that."
[04:40] You know, people think that just because you run shoutouts, you're going to make a ton of money, which is not the truth. So content really is my 80/20.
[04:50] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Content Strategy = Paid Ads Strategy"
NÚMEROS: Top, Middle, Bottom of Funnel
VISUAL: Comparação lado a lado: Paid Ads (TOFU/MOFU/BOFU) vs Instagram Content (mesmo conceito estratégico)
[05:00] Um, and it's not just like posting content. To me, I look at it similar to like paid ads, right? On paid ads obviously have like top of funnel, middle of the funnel, bottom of the funnel and you really try to like kind of be strategic with what you say and like who you speak to.
[05:15] We do kind of the same within that because at this point I believe that Instagram is so intelligent that based on you know the visuals you show, the words you use and whatnot it kind of the algorithm will manually you know redistribute who actually sees your content.
[05:35] Second key component for us is DMs. So that's really my key efficiency lever. Um, essentially like if we get a thousand DMs, the difference between, you know, six, seven, and eight figure brands is what percentage of those thousand DMs you actually turn into not only book calls, but also you turn into high-paying clients.
[06:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "DM Tool = Buyer Intent Creator"
NÚMEROS: 1,000 DMs → conversão em calls e clientes
VISUAL: Funil de conversão mostrando nurturing pesado vs. push direto para call
[06:10] Really for us, we use a DM tool or a DM tool really interestingly because we actually heavily nurture people in there as opposed to most people who just like shove them through onto a call and we actually use that to create buyers intent which helps us actually create like basically like get a higher show up rate and also convert at a much higher scale.
[06:30] And lastly which is shout outs. I'm not going to speak about that too much to be honest with you because that's my business partner's forte. Um and yeah I don't want to say anything that's false but the really big core three are the content and the DMs because shout outs they just amplify stuff.
[06:50] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Shoutouts = Amplifier (Not Magic)"
NÚMEROS: $1M gasto em ad ruim = resultados ruins
VISUAL: Analogia visual: Facebook Ad ruim + budget = falha | Conteúdo orgânico bom + shoutouts = sucesso amplificado
[07:00] So let's say if you have a Facebook ad that's like an absolute turd, you can spend a million dollars on it and you're going to get turd results. Same thing here. If you have an organic piece of content that does extremely well, you just amplify the impact of it by redistributing it onto theme pages.
[07:20] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "What Most People Do (WRONG)"
NÚMEROS: Objetivo: $100K
VISUAL: Fluxo de erro comum: Copiar Hormozi/Alex Becker → Tentar copiar conteúdo → Perceber que precisa destruir Lamborghini → Não pode pagar → Spam CTAs → Talvez $100K, talvez não
[07:30] So really what most people do and I think this just kind of has to be said is like they look up Iman Hormozi the Alex Becker or whoever then they literally just like scrape the best performing content try to copy it until they realize it takes trashing a Lambo and then they're like ah can't afford that.
[07:50] From there they literally just spam a bunch of CTAs or DM ads and then rinse and repeat might hit 100K might not hit 100K and then they look pretty damn stupid because they just copy it.
[08:05] The biggest thing that I want to kind of point out with that is you don't just want to copy something because it goes viral, right? I was saying this I think to Brett before I was like his strategy is incredibly effective, but for someone who's trying to target like 35-year-old boomers or like corporate employees, it would probably be a bit of a mismatch.
[08:30] Vice versa, if we're trying to talk about like, I don't know, wealth creation methods to a 16-year-old, it would probably not convert as well.
[08:40] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Content DON'Ts"
NÚMEROS: N/A
VISUAL: Lista com X vermelho: ❌ Copiar conteúdo viral sem contexto, ❌ Ser vanilla/modelar outros, ❌ Depender só de lifestyle/motivação
[08:45] So really a few dos and don'ts that I want to go through is just, you know, don't just copy content because it's, you know, viral. Actually create content or focus on content that speaks to like your ICP and actually attracts someone that's going to buy.
[09:00] I don't really want to have followers. I would personally just like to have a bunch of buyers.
[09:10] Second of all, it's kind of like a lot of brands are very vanilla and they just model after others, you know. Um, really what I like to do is kind of be unique in terms of your story, your opinion, and just kind of how you frame it.
[09:25] I think, you know, Brian here's a great example where like I see viral stuff that he takes and it just adds his own little twist to it, which is kind of what makes him stand out in his market.
[09:40] And then obviously and last one is like just relying on like lifestyle and motivation stuff. So, if you just use a bunch of like lifestyle flexes or like, you know, kind of show off how rich you are. I just personally believe if you're trying to target a more sophisticated market, you're not going to really stand out.
[10:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Content DOs - What Works"
NÚMEROS: 1 post de alta qualidade por dia, proporção 3:1 (valor:pitch)
VISUAL: Lista com checkmarks verdes: ✓ Liderar com valor/personalidade/história, ✓ 1 conteúdo de alta qualidade/dia, ✓ Brand como conversa (não billboard)
[10:10] What we like to do instead is we like to lead with like value, personality, and a lot of story. Because in my eyes, if somebody buys through a brand, it's because they really resonate with someone's story.
[10:25] They think that, wow, that's an interesting like tactical or like, you know, just like step-by-step insight or they just really resonate with someone's point of view.
[10:40] And then obviously, personally, I hate spamming three times a day reels. I think it just really lowers the quality. I'm pretty big on just having one high quality piece of content a day.
[11:00] That is again unless you have like a massive production team and you're able to you know really produce at scale. I just think spamming quantity over quality is a one-way street to make your brand stay small forever.
[11:20] And lastly, again this goes without saying but it's kind of what also Brad mentioned earlier is treating your brand like a billboard is not really going to make you sales.
[11:30] You know, the goal is to do it in like a way where people feel like you're giving them, you know, three times to three out of four times value and then one time you actually ask for a pitch.
[11:45] And even when you ask for a pitch, there's like a tasteful way to do it in a way where they not really feel sold too, but if more feel like they get value out of messaging you and then therefore get sold.
[12:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Strategic Content Funnel"
NÚMEROS: 1M-2M+ alcance (TOFU)
VISUAL: Três camadas de funil: TOFU (Alcance massivo 1M-2M+) → MOFU (Nurturing tático/quebra objeções) → BOFU (CTAs/DMs)
[12:10] Now again, I just I just believe in the content funnel not just being something is like any content is good content. I believe you need to be really strategic with it.
[12:20] So when we run a content strategy for clients, what we do is we kind of segregated into three buckets which is top of funnel aka how can we reach like you know a million 2 million plus people off of our target market.
[12:40] And then obviously middle of funnel will be how can we like tactically or like through personality stuff nurture those people and get them into a realm where they're like wow okay I've learned something new I've overcome an objection or like I've broken down a mental barrier and we just kind of ascend them in the buyer process.
[13:00] And then obviously with call to actions in the bottom of the funnel get them into the glory DMs.
[13:10] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "3 Types of Content We Use"
NÚMEROS: 80% das vendas vêm de threads/carousels, 10 slides de leitura
VISUAL: Três tipos destacados: 1. Reels (tráfego/awareness), 2. Threads/Carousels (80% das vendas), 3. Stories (personalidade/behind-the-scenes)
[13:15] Again for us we just use three types of content very very simple which is number one reels great great traffic mechanism for us just get a bunch of awareness you know really kind of get people in grow the audience.
[13:30] And also when you do comments CTAs and reels which is like we'll cover in a bit that gives you you know some colder leads I would say that you can then nurture and convert.
[13:45] My favorite piece of content to be quite frank are threads or like carousels whatever you guys call them they're just the absolute best because those are really the ones that make 80% of the sales.
[14:00] Because when someone slides for like 10 slides and they read all the text, they're pretty sophisticated and pretty like much more nurtured than someone who watched like a 30-second reel.
[14:15] And you know, on threads as well, we really like to break down some more complex topics. So on investment offer, we would discuss like tax strategies or, you know, some really like technical stuff and people would come through there and be like, "Wow, like you know, I'm sold like how much is the program?"
[14:35] And those are some of the easiest deals our clients can close.
[14:40] And the last thing is just more like stories. Again, I think stories are a great tool that once someone follows you, they'll just be shown, you know, few content pieces and as they see more of your content, they'll be shown your stories.
[15:00] And on your stories, it's just about you being a cool person, right? Because there's like a gazillion Airbnb coaches, there's like, you know, more fitness coaches or like, you know, any of those type of dudes.
[15:15] The real differentiator between you and them is like sort of like who you are as a person. And you can show that fairly simply through like your stories, you know, again, documenting your life, sharing some behind-the-scenes stuff.
[15:35] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Proven Content Topics"
NÚMEROS: 10 recursos por cliente (ideal)
VISUAL: Lista de tópicos de conteúdo comprovados com categorias e exemplos
[15:40] Now, again, if you want to screenshot this, there's some proven content topics. I don't really want to dwell on this too much because I'm on the call from Jeremy.
[15:55] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "2 Ways to Generate Leads"
NÚMEROS: N/A
VISUAL: Dois caminhos: 1. Comment CTA (recurso grátis → DMs → conversão) vs. 2. Direct DM CTA ("DM palavra X" → conversa direta de vendas)
[16:00] Um, again, we just generate leads two simple ways. We just have a comment CTA, which is like we give away a free resource. You know, we have like 10 resources per client in ideal scenario.
[16:15] From there, we get them into DMs where we start the sort of like the conversion process or just the more direct one where you tell, hey, just DM us the word freedom and let's actually have a chat.
[16:30] The reason I differentiate them is because like the comment CTA is a bit more sneaky, you know, it's just a bit more you get a resource first and then from there we literally just try to shove them straight into a sales conversation so they don't get lost.
[16:45] Whereas with the DM CTA is a bit more direct response.
[16:50] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "DMs - The Most Important Tool"
NÚMEROS: 80%+ show rates, $2,400+ por call agendada, 99% usando errado
VISUAL: Destaque dramático enfatizando que DMs são o diferencial mais subestimado
[17:00] Now, the DMs is really like the one thing I want to cover here more intensely because I think that's the one tool that I think 99% of people are using the wrong way.
[17:10] Um, and so really that's the tool that helps us not only scale clients up because we have like a much higher conversion and like a higher show up rate, but it just gets it just allows us to avoid speaking with like tire kickers or like people who have no money.
[17:30] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "What Most People Do WRONG in DMs"
NÚMEROS: $2/hora VA
VISUAL: Lista de erros comuns com X vermelho: ❌ VA barato ($2/hr India/Filipinas), ❌ Script rígido copy-paste, ❌ Automações excessivas, ❌ Follow-ups desesperados, ❌ Tratar setters mal
[17:35] So, what most people do, and I really am against this, they hire like a cheap India VA or like a Filipino VA for $2 an hour and they're just like, "Hey, just follow this like copy paste script and like absolutely like ramsack the out of the DMs."
[17:55] Like, I personally hate that because that's literally like a one-way shop to, you know, failure in my eyes.
[18:05] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "What We Do INSTEAD"
NÚMEROS: UK/US setters, comissão apenas (performance-based)
VISUAL: Lista de práticas corretas com checkmarks: ✓ UK/US setters, ✓ Performance-based pay, ✓ Adaptive scripts, ✓ Minimal automation, ✓ Creative follow-ups, ✓ Setters + Closers como tag team
[18:10] I personally really like having UK or US setters and basically just put them on percentage only basis. As always, if somebody has a commission or like performance incentive, they'll likely do a lot better work.
[18:30] Um, second thing as well is you know having a rigid copy paste script. I mean I've seen so many people give scripts away. It's literally like how much money do you have and like you know where are you located from?
[18:45] It's like it's so rigid it doesn't allow adaptation. You know what we like to do is we call it an adaptive script. So like based on where a conversation flows we adapt.
[19:00] So if somebody gives us two-word responses we'll give them a massive ego check. Whereas if somebody's like very open we'll be like more excited.
[19:15] Or if someone comes in and is a bit more like reserved, we skip financial qualifier questions because we know it just tends to, you know, scare people away.
[19:30] And so, I just like being more responsive and adaptive because that allows us to actually push more people through.
[19:40] Again, here, I hate automations outside of like opening messages and maybe a follow-up sequence. I don't use automations inside of ManyChat at all. I just try to use them only when needed.
[20:00] Same thing here, follow-up. I know Jeremy agrees with me here when I say I hate people that follow up like did you see this? Did you get abducted by an alien? Question mark, did you forget how to read or like any of that stuff.
[20:20] Like I just honestly it just it just devalues you as a brand and it makes you look pretty damn stupid. Um I like you know what's called a more personalized like creative follow-up.
[20:35] In terms of if somebody admits in a conversation that they struggle let's say on a weight loss offer they struggle with like dieting. I'd be like, "Hey, by the way, I just released this YouTube video on like this XYZ diet. You should really check it out."
[20:50] And that, you know, for us at least, reactivates a bunch of conversations really, really well. And, you know, as a whole, helps us as well to book more calls.
[21:05] And the last thing is just a lot of people that we've worked with sadly, they treat setters a bit like, you know, the bottom of the earth's scum. Um, and they're literally just like churn and burn them and you know, just pretty much treat them like idiots.
[21:25] I kind of flip the script around. I would give that treatment to closers, but really give the setters a lot more authorities and rights because what I found is that when you kind of combine setters and closers together, they kind of work like a tag team.
[21:45] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Setter + Closer Tag Team Example"
NÚMEROS: $50K-$100K/mês em conversões extras de deals marginais
VISUAL: Fluxo de colaboração: Closer identifica lead hesitante → Setter recebe alerta → Setter "cozinha" lead nas DMs → Lead converte
[21:50] So on this one particular offer that we had for an Amazon brand offer, the setter actually was almost running the sales team and what he would do is like the sales teams would fill out call forms and they would hit him up be like, "Hey, cook this guy in the DMs because he's basically like just being like a bit of a pussy."
[22:10] And so the guy would go back be like, "Hey man, I really thought you were about it." And so the guy would be like, "Oh, damn. This person just reached out to me and like, you know, said I was about it." And would actually end up converting.
[22:30] And so giving the setters a bit more rights and kind of combining them with the closers has been such a powerful move to convert a much much higher percentage of like leads.
[22:45] Does anything so far make sense or any glaring questions?
[22:50] [Pergunta da audiência sobre percentual de conversão de deals que não fecharam inicialmente]
[23:00] No, but I would say fairly confidently we consistently close like an extra 50 to 100k per month on specific deals on fringe deals where like the setter pushes them through.
[23:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "DM Setting Process Framework"
NÚMEROS: EQ alto necessário
VISUAL: Framework mental e processo de setting (não script exato)
[23:20] All right, so I'm going to go through sort of the setting process. I'm going to go through the entire script. Again, I'm I hate giving away exact messages, so I'm just going to really talk through like the framework and like sort of more the mental model that we have around DM setting.
[23:40] The big thing I'm on when especially when we're working with setters is like I want people that have like high EQ. The reason for that is because when somebody texts through DMs, there's a lot that goes lost in translation.
[24:00] And obviously sometimes text messages can reveal a lot about a person and you need someone that can actually like understand that and react accordingly.
[24:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Phase 1: Discovery"
NÚMEROS: 2-10 nível de interesse inicial
VISUAL: Processo de descoberta: Avaliar interesse → Filtrar por país/qualificação → Entender nível de conhecimento (modelo + criador)
[24:20] So the first things for us is like discovery. Um and obviously whenever somebody messages us a DM, we just usually gauge their interest, right?
[24:35] In terms of, hey, if it's an Airbnb offer, we would say, hey, are you already new to this or are you already a host? That way, we know, okay, cool, this might be a, you know, baseline package or a more upsell package person.
[24:55] Um, and then from there, what we really try to do off the bat is we try to either drastically disqualify in terms of if, you know, our offer can only be sold in the US, we just filter by country and then obviously disqualify from there.
[25:15] But then realistically, we want to know their sort of interest level, right? I think Jeremy spoke about this in a call and pretty much it's kind of like within the first two three days you understand how much does that person know about the business model or the opportunity I'm trying to sell them and how much do they know about me as the creator.
[25:40] You know one of our sales teams we had this issue where like they would jump on and they'd be like oh so um what does Kyle actually do and it was like well first the guy's name is Ben and he actually does this weird thing.
[26:00] And so we kind of realized that if you just let anyone through who doesn't have any awareness on the model or the person your sales team is pretty much set up for failure.
[26:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Gauge Initial Interest = Vehicle Understanding"
NÚMEROS: 2/10 interesse → recursos → 5-6/10, ou 5/10 → recursos → 6-7/10
VISUAL: Escala de interesse vinculada ao entendimento do veículo/modelo de negócio
[26:20] So what we do here um which is kind of like in the gauge initial step is we're really trying to understand like how much do they know about the vehicle because for me the level of interest is all dependent on the vehicle understanding.
[26:40] If they understand what the vehicle looks like it's like oh yeah like I'm really interested in like business acquisitions or like you know I always wanted to start a fitness coaching offer. At that point, I can go and say, "Okay, cool. That person gets it." And then I kind of ascend them.
[27:00] If I understand that they have a really, you know, they have zero idea what's going on, I would kind of just give them a tool. Or if I felt like they just given me a halfass answer, we would actually go and ego check them.
[27:20] Again, I always like that because it once makes the brand stand out because you're not just like desperate to get him on a call, but you kind of like check him a bit and it usually raises interest.
[27:35] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Ego Check Example"
NÚMEROS: $10K close em 2 dias após ego check
VISUAL: Exemplo de conversa: Lead dá respostas de uma palavra → Setter: "Se você gosta de ser pobre, me avise" → Lead envia essay de 400 palavras → $10K PIFF
[27:40] Um, on one particular offer, I remember this, we had someone who's a bit more like on the red pill side and you know, people would just give him like one-word answers.
[27:55] And so eventually the setter would just be like, "Hey, if you enjoy being poor, you know, then just let me know." And the person would be like, "What? Like what? What are you talking about?"
[28:10] He's like, "Well, if you give me one word answers, I can't help you." And the guy would literally send us like, you know, a 400-word essay about his life and, you know, was like a 10k PIFF two days later.
[28:30] And so we really try to on that frame we try to be really strict in terms of like we're not moving on unless they give us satisfactory answers in terms of like what they're struggling with or like how much they do or do not understand.
[28:50] And then at the end of this we're really understanding like okay cool they have you know some form of lack of knowledge either in the tactical side of things. So what is the vehicle about or who is the person and like how has this vehicle changed his life?
[29:10] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Resource Distribution Strategy"
NÚMEROS: 24 horas para consumir recurso (não 4 horas)
VISUAL: Timeline: Enviar recurso → Esperar 24h (não pressionar) → Follow-up: "Como foi o vídeo?"
[29:15] And then from there, we just have like a different set of like resources, which I'll cover in a bit. And we basically just send out resources.
[29:30] Again, our goal is if we identify someone as a two out of 10 interested, we try to see, okay, which resource gets him to like a five or six, which is what he needs to kind of go to the next phase.
[29:45] Or if someone's a five, we're like, okay, cool. We just got to get him to that six or seven mark so we can ascend. And really, that kind of concludes the discovery where we kind of know, okay, cool. They have the resources now. And you know, now they're kind of nurturing themselves.
[30:05] What we do is we used to wait two to five days. Now we kind of shorten it down to like 24 hours, but realistically we kind of actually give them 24 hours to consume the resource.
[30:20] Point being is because we don't try to shove it down their throats. You know, most of our clients, they're like really successful with their actual business or like you know, mildly successful.
[30:35] And basically what we want to do is we want to not take that away because if you're like after four hours like hey did you watch the video or like are you ready to book a call? It just makes you look really desperate and I think you lose a lot of like the status delta that you could have.
[30:55] And so after 24 hours we literally just ask them like hey how do you like the video? You know how was this resource? Did you gain some understanding out of it?
[31:10] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Phase 2: Game Plan Assessment"
NÚMEROS: N/A
VISUAL: Perguntas estratégicas: "Qual seu plano agora?" → Avaliar clareza → Se vago: questionar hesitação → Se muito certo: criar dúvida
[31:15] And from there we try to see whether they watch it or what they got out of it. And then from there we kind of ask a game plan.
[31:25] Now what we do is we pretty much go through like hey what exactly is your plan now? Or you know in the case of the vending machine offer we would go cool which state are you going to set it up in.
[31:40] And you know they either say oh really clearcut plan like I'm going to set it up there in this city and like I'm super excited about it or they would be a bit more vague.
[31:55] And again there we kind of check them if they're not 100% sure we're like okay what makes you hesitant or whatever and we just kind of give them a few little nudges so they're super clear.
[32:10] And then from there we're like cool do you feel like you're set up for yourself or like you know have you seen like what happens when you don't know the right things and we just try to create a bit of doubt if they're too certain, right?
[32:30] So that they're like, "Ooh, maybe I actually need some help." Most of the time though, they're like, "Yeah, I'm pretty sure, but I just, you know, I have some questions."
[32:45] And at that point, we just kind of pitch our call because what we want to do is we want them to start already thinking about doing this.
[33:00] The moment somebody thinks about like, "Oh, I'm going to do this or like this is the state I'm going to set up my vending machine," they're already like to a degree they have a very high intent of taking action on it, which is when I think it's very suitable to kind of hand them over to the sales team.
[33:20] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Phase 3: Booking the Call"
NÚMEROS: N/A
VISUAL: Três caminhos: 1. Interesse claro → Enviar link, 2. Cético → Call out → Objeção real → Responder/Recursos, 3. Pergunta preço → Depende: produto barato (enviar link) vs high ticket (criar razão para call)
[33:25] And from there, it's very very simple. Um, you know, if they show clear interest in booking a call, we send the link, we book the call.
[33:40] And then if they're a bit more skeptical, you know, what we do there is we either call them out, be like, "Okay, cool. Well, you know, in that case, you're not very serious about like losing XYZ pounds or like, you know, making XYZ money."
[34:00] And, you know, from there, usually that kind of gives the objection that they really have or the real concern, which again, either we just answer it through a message or we just give them more resources, which like that usually does the trick and leads us to booking a call.
[34:20] Or what some people do is they ask about the price, right? And obviously it's a bit of a tricky one. What we like to do is if somebody's like super dead sold in some instances and like a cheaper product, we just send the payment link and we just give them the price.
[34:40] Or a more higher ticket one, we're like, well, really depends and we just kind of make up some form of reason as to why they should jump on the call.
[34:55] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Nurture Assets - Game Changers"
NÚMEROS: 9 horas de conteúdo consumido overnight → 3 closes em 1 semana
VISUAL: Lista de assets poderosos com ícones e descrições
[35:00] And so with that said, the one thing I want to cover here is some of the nurture assets we use because I will swear down the nurture assets, they have made the world of difference for us.
[35:15] And the number one being a webinar recording. I love webinars. I love webinar recordings. We literally just cut off the pitch part. Like we just cut that out and then just upload it. Um, that one is just awesome.
[35:35] Um, if any of you guys have done a three or 4 day challenge, there are awesome too. Like we just cut them all together into one YouTube video, private it or send that out. People love that.
[35:50] Um, Trey over there, he actually runs a sales team on one of my offers. They actually had the idea to do this randomly and we had like three closes in a week where someone booked a call. They watched nine hours overnight of like challenge videos. They came on and PIFFed.
[36:15] We were like, "Ah, now we know why America has an economy problem."
[36:20] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Asset #2: Free Course (YouTube)"
NÚMEROS: N/A
VISUAL: "What to do, NOT how to do" - Vender no veículo, não dar step-by-step completo
[36:25] Um, the second thing that we really like is like a free course. So, it's like a YouTube video, right? The typical like kind of what to do, not how to do video.
[36:40] I really like them because it just kind of does the job of like getting you sold on the vehicle. I just like sending out a free YouTube video because then they can consume it. They might subscribe to YouTube and go into the YouTube ecosystem.
[37:00] But most importantly, they get sold on the vehicle, why it's a great opportunity, how it can solve XYZ issue and just addresses a bunch of objections as well.
[37:15] [Pergunta da audiência sobre exemplo de "what to do, not how to do"]
[37:20] So for example, we would go in, let's say an agency, right? I would go and run a free agency course and I would go through, yeah, so first you basically like do outreach and I would cover a lot on the outreach side of things.
[37:40] But I wouldn't go through you send exactly this message, use exactly this tool. I would just the stuff that you teach in your paid programs, you just kind of want to give the overview, not the nitty-gritty step by step.
[37:55] I've found that a lot of people, they just like that's what they really want. They don't want to be told sort of like how to do it. They just want to be told what to do at first and then obviously they you know just kind of hear it after they buy.
[38:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Asset #3: Objection Handling Videos"
NÚMEROS: 50 minutos de vídeo sobre tariffs = múltiplos deals fechados
VISUAL: Processo: Sync semanal com sales team → Identificar objeções recorrentes → Cliente grava vídeo específico → Distribuir em thread/story/DMs
[38:20] Next what we do is we love specific objection handling videos. So for example on an offer where we work with Josh what we love to do on the weekly sync calls with the sales team is we kind of ask what are the objections what are the sales reps struggling with or like what's recurring right?
[38:45] And what came out with one of the offers was that people were like, "Oh, the tariffs are like, you know, kind of us." Like a lot of people are a bit iffy. They're like a bit unsure.
[39:00] So, we immediately like went to town, client recorded like a 50-minute video like how economy, like how, you know, struggles in the economy are actually great for XYZ business.
[39:15] We ran up like a thread, a story in like a video. And like I think we had a bunch of deals come through right afterwards because those objections were handled.
[39:30] And even in the DMs, you know, if somebody was uncertain, we would say like, "You worried about the tariff?" They'd be like, "Yes, yes." And we're like, "Well, watch this video. It'll sort your stuff."
[39:45] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Asset #4: In-Depth Client Case Studies"
NÚMEROS: N/A
VISUAL: "Dummy Theory" - Usar case studies de pessoas que o avatar se sente superior para criar viés de confiança
[39:50] Um, next thing as well is we like to do in-depth client case studies. In the client case studies, don't cancel me for it. But what I like to do is I like to use the dummy theory.
[40:05] So, if we have clients that, you know, let's say, for example, they have a primarily like white male demographic. We might use a case study of people where they're like, "Oh, I'm better than this person or like I'm way I have a higher chance of being successful than that."
[40:25] And like we just send that case study out because they immediately go, "Oh, like I'm going to be more successful than that person. Therefore, this is a great opportunity for me."
[40:40] And so that's really what we like to do because on that side, it just kind of creates bias because obviously I think human tendency is to overestimate oneself.
[40:55] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Asset #5: Niche-Specific Podcasts"
NÚMEROS: 50%+ close rate após 2 semanas de podcasts
VISUAL: Impacto dramático de podcasts: familiaridade profunda → leads chegam "cashiering" → "Eu amo o que você disse no episódio X"
[41:00] And the last thing that I love is niche specific podcasts. Um, you know, back when we worked with a client, um, Rob Oliver, he went on this random podcast run I wasn't even aware of.
[41:15] And within literally 2 weeks of some of these podcasts being dripped out, you know, the close rate shot up to like over 50%. And I was like, yo, guys, what the going on?
[41:30] And, you know, they just kind of hit me up and we're like, "Yeah, like, dude, we just been sending these podcasts and like these people are showing up and they're like, dude, I love what you said here and there and you know, I know all these things."
[41:45] And it was more like yeah like as Jeremy calls it like cashiering you know.
[42:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Pre-Call Asset (Notion Page)"
NÚMEROS: 9 horas de challenge content, envio automático em 10 segundos
VISUAL: Notion page estruturada: FAQ completa → Testimonials (texto/vídeo) → Challenge gratuito 3 dias → Vídeos táticos super específicos
[42:05] And so I love going niche specific podcasts and then what we usually do which is like the coolest thing ever is we turn everything into a pre-call asset.
[42:20] Again shout out Trey for putting this one together but what we do is we just create like a common FAQ section which is like every single question you usually get asked on a sales call or any objection you could have and then you just answer it there.
[42:40] Obviously, you click the arrows and then you get the results. And then you have testimonials. Obviously, a bunch of testimonials, texts, actual videos, whatever. I just love to throw in as much social proof as possible so we can really flex the results.
[43:00] And then just as a free bonus, obviously a 3-day buy a business challenge, which is nine hours of straight yapping.
[43:10] And then on top of that, below, we would use like super tactical videos. So, it could be something as like if, let's say, you're doing Facebook ads, right? Be like a Facebook ads guide or like how to avoid, you know, the iOS update or like you got you you guys name it.
[43:30] And so those assets have been super super key because what they do immediately is like they address so many questions and they really create like strong familiarity.
[43:45] [Pergunta: Você envia isso para o prospect?]
[43:50] Yeah, we send that out. So yeah, it's a Notion doc. We send that out immediately after they booked the call. We're like, "Yo, dude. Just saw you booked the call."
[44:05] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Zapier Automation Magic"
NÚMEROS: 10 segundos após booking
VISUAL: Fluxo automático: Link enviado → Zapier captura ManyChat user ID → Atualiza call → Auto-envia Notion em 10s
[44:10] Um, one thing I haven't added here because it's too technical, but we basically found this new, I think it's like a Zapier automation where if we send a link out, it automatically attracts the ManyChat's user ID to the call link.
[44:30] It zaps it into obviously the calls, updates it and automatically sends out the moment they book within 10 seconds like, "Hey, just saw you booked the call. Here's just some resource to go through. That'd be really awesome."
[44:50] That has been just killer for us because now they actually go through it and then really what we do is in the DMs the setter kind of follows up and like yo I know you have your call in two days just wanted to see if you had any questions on the resources.
[45:10] And a lot of times they would share like more concerns or like things they're not 100% sure about and then from there we're able to objection handle the out of them already before the call.
[45:25] [Pergunta: Por que Notion e não landing page?]
[45:30] Yeah, I don't like landing pages personally. Um but also too I just think Notion's very native. I just found personally like even on content like I like stuff to look native and organic, not overedited.
[45:50] And so like I like native looking stuff because I think that makes people less feel sold to.
[46:00] Yeah. Any questions on that?
[46:10] [Pergunta sobre enviar podcast]
[46:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Podcast Follow-up Script"
NÚMEROS: N/A
VISUAL: Mensagem transparente: "Vai ter venda, mas quero que você tenha todas as informações para tomar a decisão certa - mesmo se comprar sem informação, é decisão errada"
[46:20] Yeah. It'd be like, "Hey, by the way, if you just want to learn a bit more, you know, there's some really cool podcasts I've done where I think a lot of stuff that you would want to know before the call, you know, you can walk through so that on the call, we can just be super tactical and see how to implement this for you."
[46:45] I just liked it. I really took this from Hormozi like even on like breakdown videos or whatnot. I like to be very direct in terms of like, hey, look, there is going to be something to sell, right?
[47:00] But I want to make sure that like you have all the information so you make the right decision. Because even if you buy but you don't have enough information, it's the wrong decision. I don't want you to do that.
[47:15] And I think it just kind of gives you that extra bit of like authority and like, oh, that person actually cares, you know?
[47:30] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Efficiency Metrics We Track"
NÚMEROS: <$1-2 cost per follower ideal, <$0.50 em escala, 10%+ lead-to-call ratio, 75-85%+ show rate, $1,389 avg cash per call, até $2,900 em alguns clientes
VISUAL: Dashboard de métricas principais com benchmarks
[47:35] Sweet. So, I want to go through lastly just some metrics for efficiency. I'm a big efficiency guy. Uh, and sometimes sales people hate me for it because the thing with organic, especially when we run shoutouts, it's not like Facebook ads where you just press a button, you get like infinite scale, you know?
[48:00] It's like you need to be really really efficient with like what you do.
[48:10] So, first things first, and this is obviously more if you run collaboration posts, shout outs, like any form of like paid, you know, follower acquisitions is you really want to measure sort of your cost per follower.
[48:30] For us, you know, if you spend more than one or two dollars for a follower, I think it's pretty you know, and it's not really going to give you those outsized returns that I personally look for. For us at scale anything below a dollar or you know even ideally below 50 cents is kind of what we are looking for.
[48:55] Next thing is like lead to call ratio. This is by far my number one ratio that I look for. So lead to call ratio is for your DM setters. It's basically like what percentage of you know inbound DMs are actually booking a call.
[49:15] So if let's say you get a thousand inbound DMs you know if you book only 50 of them so 5% it's pretty. I personally, and I just want to say this very respectfully, I do not give a how many of those are like promotion invites, how many of them are from like non-buyer countries.
[49:40] Like I don't care whether we've tracked it very specifically or not, the numbers have really stayed the same. So, for us, it's a good metric is anything above 10%.
[50:00] If you're running like DM funnel ads where like you run ads and they go straight into the DMs, I would reckon this has to be as high as like 20% because you're getting a much more higher and much more qualified audience.
[50:20] In the next thing is show up rate. Again, it's just a percentage of people that show up. You know, to me I love how I think Jeremy said this once like it measures how much people give a about the call and the offer.
[50:40] Again, this really measures to me not only how effective the offer is, but it's also how good of a job have the DM setters done.
[50:55] It's easy to go and say, "Yes, sir." Like, you know, do you have 5K? Great. Okay. Do you have a credit card? Great. You seem to have a pulse, so let's book you on a call. You're probably not going to have a very high show rate, you know.
[51:15] But I kind of like to be very diligent on that because that's one role that I have the setters own because for me, I want to see show rates up 75% plus and above.
[51:30] Last month for two of our clients, we had like an 85% plus show rate, which has been, you know, pretty killer.
[51:45] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Cash Per Call - The Josh Troy Metric"
NÚMEROS: $1,000 benchmark tradicional, $1,389 média atual, até $2,900 em cliente específico, $700 = problema (60% não completam payment plans)
VISUAL: Métrica composta revelando: show rate + cash collection + qualidade do sales team
[51:50] Um, but really those numbers I kind of need to see because it shows that we do a good job nurturing and then, you know, kind of converting on the same time.
[52:05] Second metric, again, shout out Josh, that's a metric I learned from working with Josh on some deals, is basically like cash per call, right? Josh calls it something more complicated, but I'm going to just keep it cash per call.
[52:25] And it's basically how much money we make per call booked. I think traditionally, for those of you who track that metric, I think $1,000 is a good metric.
[52:40] Historically, this year at least across all of our clients, we've been collecting an average of $1,389 per booked call have seen as high as like $2,900 on one particular client account.
[53:00] So for us really that's one metric I'm an absolute stickler for because it shows not only is the show up rate good or bad. It also shows how good are we at collecting you know cash and also do we have a bunch of sales teams that just collect like split pays right.
[53:25] We have one client very long-term client of mine good friend but like he just has a history of hiring absolute potatoes for sales management and you know he was telling me he bought this new sales manager is awesome you know we're closing 30%.
[53:45] I'm like, "Yeah, we're collecting $700, you know, per booked call. It's pretty damn poor." And so, we started looking into it and we just started seeing like, you know, they're collecting like $500 split payments and like 60% of those people don't complete their payment plans.
[54:10] So, we're like, well, maybe we should uh replace a sales team, you know, like actually start having them push for a PIFF or like just, you know, less payment plans or whatever they were doing.
[54:30] And so, really, I like that metric because it just kind of shows the overall efficiency of the sales process.
[54:40] And then lastly, close rate. I mean, I don't since working with Josh, to be quite transparent, I don't look at close rate anymore, I just look at it more in relation to the cash per call metric.
[55:00] I think that's just super important because I like a high close rate, but at the end of the day, I just know how many calls we can book. And if we can have a 50% close rate, if we collect $500 per call, doesn't really do me any good.
[55:20] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "For Paid Ad People: Content Multiplier Effect"
NÚMEROS: 2.5x ROAS → 2-3x ROAS (dobrou/triplicou) em 3-4 semanas, mesmo ad spend
VISUAL: Caso de estudo: Cliente apenas ads → Implementou sistema de conteúdo → ROAS multiplicado sem aumentar budget
[55:25] So, those are really the metrics that I look for and kind of look at to audit, you know, obviously cost per follower, how are we performing? But on the lead to call ratio and like cash per call also like how is the sales team performing.
[55:45] And so the last thing I want to cover just briefly is an observation that I've made on clients that heavily rely on ads.
[56:00] I had a consulting client that was running primarily ads and you know he was basically like running like a 2.5x ROAS and like 30k in spend and within you know three or four weeks of implementing our system he came to me he's like dude I still spend the same amount but you know we've almost doubled or tripled our ROAS.
[56:25] And I was like very interesting and so what happened was obviously all these people that you know see your ads they might click on your profile and then if it's just a bunch of pictures of like you and your dog or like you know you and your family like that might be cool but it doesn't really create buying intent.
[56:50] What happened was with him is he actually started to put a bunch of like tactical guides. He was in like some trading style, whatever. He had a bunch of tactical guides, you know, his origin story and just kind of like framing how this trading strategy that guy had was a unique opportunity.
[57:15] And off of that alone, a lot of people that saw the ads and clicked on it actually ended up going into the DMs and converted because they were like, "Ah, this actually pretty cool. I want to learn a bit more about it."
[57:35] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "3-Step Content Blueprint for Ad People"
NÚMEROS: 5-7 dias de conteúdo, stories diárias
VISUAL: Três passos simples: 1. Contratar DM setter qualificado, 2. Implementar cronograma 5-7 dias de conteúdo, 3. Postar Instagram stories diariamente
[57:40] And so really there's three steps involved here, which is number one, hire a DM setter and hire a good one. You know, if you need help with hiring one, I have, you know, a few networks of DM setters that I personally use, so you can just message me about that.
[58:00] Step two, you know, just implement a simple 5 to 7 day content schedule.
[58:10] And number three, post daily Instagram stories. There's nothing worse than if I click on a profile and it's just like empty and no personality on there.
[58:25] At that point, we're back to square one where every Facebook ad person uses the classic Cole Gordon script and, you know, looks the exact same. I like to kind of stand out and be a bit more unique, which is how you're probably going to scale.
[58:45] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "5-7 Day Content Schedule Breakdown"
NÚMEROS: 2x por dia stories documentando, 1x por semana Q&A, 1-2x por semana resultados/dicas
VISUAL: Calendário semanal com tipos de conteúdo: FAQ tactical guide, breakdown de resultado, objection handling criativa, origin story, picture dump, tactical thread
[58:50] So, frequently asked question, you know, providing like a tactical guide on like how your methods work or like how you get your results. You break down an outcome that could be like how I spend my income, how much money I made, you know.
[59:10] Um a very creative objection handling. So, like we basically did this reel for our clients like, "Oh, oh, it's 10k booking, but how much profit?" And we would just break down exactly how much an Airbnb unit would make him profit.
[59:30] And like that was kind of cool because like people were like, "Well, why is he clowning himself?" And then they just showed in the end like it actually works.
[59:45] Or just the origin story, which again, it just kind of helps build that relationship. And then from there, picture dump. It just kind of shows a bit more of your life and the tactical thread where you might break down some key questions they have or some key concepts that might make you stand out.
[60:05] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Instagram Stories Strategy"
NÚMEROS: 2x por dia documentação, 1x por semana Q&A, 1-2x por semana wins/tips
VISUAL: Framework de stories: Document day 2x/dia (relevante ao nicho) → Q&A semanal → Tactical tips 1-2x/semana → Behind the scenes → Student wins 1x/semana (não diariamente)
[60:10] On stories. Super simple. I just like to document my days. Like document your day twice a day. Again, keep in mind that it has to be somewhat relevant. You know, if you're selling a drop shipping course, showing how you garden twice a day might not get you sales.
[60:35] Um once a week, we love doing Q&A. Q&As are awesome because you can reach out to people that ask questions and you know you essentially get to do a bunch of objection handling before they even reach out.
[60:55] And then just honestly once or twice a week sharing a result or just a tactical tip you know where you're like hey look I just had this question asked you know um here's kind of why this is this way um and then just kind of taking people behind the scenes.
[61:15] And then I personally I'm not a big fan of like just sharing student wins every day because it just makes you like an info nerd I just like to do like once a week being like hey look you know here's like an overview of what our students been doing and just casually drop a bunch of client wins.
[61:35] With that said, obviously if you guys have any questions, unless I have no more time, Jeremy, then let me know.
[61:45] [Q&A Session Begins]
[61:50] [Pergunta sobre keyword tracking em shoutouts]
[62:00] Not really. We had some clients that tried to do it. I just personally don't like doing that because it's so hard to measure. And then from a media buying side, you need to basically like create a 100 plus ad folders for every ad and change a different keyword and then like that being tracked is just it's just a bit harder to do.
[62:25] We've done this for so long to be fair. Like we've done this for 5 years. My business partner still looks at like you know a gazillion ads a day. We just kind of know at this point because we pre-vet pages quite hard that like these pages are like really good or like really bad and we just kind of know the impact and we see the swing in stuff.
[62:50] So like back in the day there was like these two pages where like if we just run shout outs on them it would just be like plus 10,000 DMs and we're like pretty good DMs, you know. Yeah. So it's like that's pretty much that.
[63:10] More of that though as like it evolves. I just really like the consistency of it you know. I just look at like a week two week horizon. If it's increasing great if not then sucks.
[63:25] [Pergunta: E você vetta cada página nova?]
[63:30] Yeah we vet them very strictly because like these will just like send you the biggest bogus insights ever. So, we vet them like, "Hey, open the photo app, then click on the third last picture, then go back on Instagram, then search this person, and then basically make it impossible to fake it."
[63:55] And guess what? If you still fake us, then congratulations to you. Like, well played, you know, like here's your $50.
[64:10] [Pergunta sobre sequência de boas-vindas para followers]
[64:15] We don't really like doing that. I think it just kind of personally I believe it just kind of devalues your brand a bit. And what we do is if we realize like organic lead flow is down tremendously. We kind of do it but more like yo just saw you followed. Thank you so much. Anything you'd like to see contentwise from me.
[64:40] That's pretty much it. Like hey where are you in your journey just want to give every follower like some massive value but just need to know where you're at.
[64:55] I just keep it super value based personally. That's how I kind of like going about sales in general but I avoid hammering every single follower with sales. I just don't think it really scales.
[65:15] [Pergunta de Brett sobre volume de DMs e capacidade de setters]
[65:20] Mr. Brett, I uh I get hundreds of DMs per day and I think our biggest bottleneck with DM setters is that they can't keep up with the volume. But the problem is like how do you keep those DM setters incentivized cuz you could obviously just hire more DM setters.
[65:40] Can you give me a ballpark of how many DMs you get a day?
[65:45] Probably like 100 to 200.
[65:50] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Skill Issue Example"
NÚMEROS: 350+ leads/dia, 37 calls agendadas/dia por 1 setter, 2 semanas consecutivas
VISUAL: Case study extremo de eficiência: 1 setter processando volume massivo sozinho
[65:55] I got a skill issue. We have a DM setter right now. I don't mean it in a bad way, but we have a DM setter right now for a client that's working through 350 plus leads every single day for the past two weeks and he's been booking like 37 calls a day by himself.
[66:20] 37 calls a day?
[66:25] Yeah.
[66:30] Problem is like the script and all because all they're doing is just trying to get the phone number from the lead and then normal setting goal. So would you say hire more DM setters?
[66:45] Yeah. If how do I keep them incentivized?
[66:50] Well, you pay them commission, right?
[67:00] [Pergunta sobre encontrar setters com EQ alto]
[67:05] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Sam Ovens Method: Opposite of Qualified Lead"
NÚMEROS: Primeiras 2 perguntas = DQ automático
VISUAL: Framework de qualificação: Definir oposto de lead qualificado → Criar perguntas DQ → Primeira mensagem ManyChat auto-DQ → Focar apenas em qualificados
[67:10] Look at like my personal brand, right? So many like people that are not bad lead quality. So what I like to do is I like to basically like play this game that Sam Ovens actually taught which is like what's the opposite of a qualified lead?
[67:30] And then you kind of figure out what are the traits and then in the setting script the first two questions are just questions that automatically disqualify the bad apples, right?
[67:45] Like the first question we use ManyChat to like send the first message. So you can automatically DQ a lot of people immediately and then from there you just focus on the qualified ones.
[68:00] So it's just like systemizing the script?
[68:05] Yeah, I think systemizing the script a bit. It's like a very personalized approach which wastes a lot of time.
[68:15] I like to balance it out. So, obviously I told you like we're pretty big on the personalized stuff, but we just kind of keep it like we avoid going into a conversation where like you know their grandma's name and like you know what street they were born on.
[68:35] Like we try not to go that far like we just keep it like more like on a conversational basis but just lead the convo because otherwise what quickly happens is leads will lead the conversation just ask you a gazillion questions.
[68:55] So you try to qualify first then?
[69:00] Yeah. Exactly.
[69:05] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Two-Tier DM System"
NÚMEROS: 3 perguntas qualificadoras
VISUAL: Sistema de dois níveis: Tier 1 "Opener" (base pay, 3 perguntas qualificadoras, filtra inbounds) → Tier 2 "Setter" (commission, apenas leads flagged/qualificados, agenda calls)
[69:10] I've got something that might be able to help you. So bro I have like two different roles in my DMs. So like I've got one person that does like DM setting that just like is like an opener.
[69:30] So essentially what they do is they take conversations that are coming in that are like just every conversation that comes in and they ask like three qualifying questions like what's your story, what got you into fitness, like you know like how long you've been working on it etc.
[69:50] And once they identify that it's like a qualified lead then they pass it to a setter and I just pay that person a base pay and all they do is handle the inbounds and then they're filtering the inbounds and they're passing the qualified ones to the appointment setters.
[70:15] So the appointment setters are just going in checking the ones that are flagged and then they're having the conversations with qualified leads so that they can just go find the qualifiers and then it gets passed on the phone and then it gets passed to the phone setter.
[70:35] So yeah, so it's like opener does like the nurturing and then it passes it to somebody that actually books the call once the phone...
[70:45] ManyChat?
[70:50] Uh no, we don't do ManyChat. It's all manual but you can also do it on ManyChat where you just then tag the opener would obviously I would say tag the setter and then take it from there.
[71:10] Try the new uh Instagram integration of GoHighLevel too but I hate GoHighLevel. Um, I think GoHighLevel is one of the crappiest softwares when it comes to DM settings.
[71:30] Sorry if there's anyone from GoHighLevel, but yeah, I personally really dislike it.
[71:40] ManyChat the best?
[71:45] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "ManyChat vs GoHighLevel"
NÚMEROS: 3,000-4,000 DMs em 1 dia (ManyChat = zero problemas), 500 mensagens GoHighLevel = 30 dias chat restricted
VISUAL: Comparação de estabilidade: ManyChat suporta volume extremo vs. GoHighLevel falha com volume moderado
[71:50] ManyChat is the best for us. We've gotten as much as like 3,000-4,000 DMs once in a day. Like this is like a year or two ago and like nothing broke.
[72:10] We have one client ever that got 500 messages on GoHighLevel and we were chat restricted for 30 days and yeah, it was not nice.
[72:30] [Pergunta sobre compensação de DM setters]
[72:35] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "DM Setter Compensation"
NÚMEROS: 3-5% cash collected, até $25K/mês para setter top (em $500K+ sales)
VISUAL: Filosofia de compensação: Pagar bem + menos setters = maior motivação e qualidade
[72:40] Um, DM setters usually we compensate anywhere. It really depends on a per client basis, but it's between 3 to 5% cash collected.
[73:00] Um, I personally like setters to be well paid and just keep less setters around so they can make much more. We have we've had setters that make as much as like 25k a month because they obviously set like, you know, basically like what was it like 500k?
[73:25] [Discussão sobre frequência de DMCTAs]
[73:30] Yeah, that's on our personal brand, we're running very frequent DMCTAs. Like every second video is a DMCTA because it drives a ton of traffic. So we'll get anywhere 500 to 1,000 DMs per day on average.
[73:50] You think that there's a problem with the amount of like the cadence of how frequently we're running DMCTAs actually in the reels because we're sending them like very similar lead magnets once they come into the DMs.
[74:10] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Lead Magnet Split Testing Strategy"
NÚMEROS: 2,000-3,000 leads re-engajados/mês com split testing
VISUAL: Benefícios de múltiplos lead magnets: Re-engajamento massivo de leads antigos + freshness para novos leads
[74:15] I like to split test lead magnets. I like a ton of lead magnets because that way we can re-engage old leads. Like we've literally started testing it and since split testing it we had on average on a client we had like two to three thousand leads re-engaged every single month just for splitting up lead magnets.
[74:45] Um on the frequency like I like to keep it three to four times a week max right but then again like I think Brian you have a CTA almost daily but you obviously post a lot more like you can obviously speak to that.
[75:05] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Brian's High-Volume CTA Strategy"
NÚMEROS: 6 posts/dia, 7 dias/semana, 1-2 CTAs/dia
VISUAL: Proporção jab-jab-jab-right hook: 6 posts diários permitem 2 CTAs sem parecer spam
[75:10] Yeah. Yeah. I do. So I post I post six times a day, seven days a week. So I do like a CTA like once or twice a day. One of them's a direct CTA like DM me for coaching. Another one's like a lead magnet like some sort of resource that my avatar will get value from.
[75:35] But I think that like I try to follow jab jab jab right hook though. So like I'm posting six times a day. So I can get away with doing two.
[75:50] Sorry for insulting your little capacity of only three times.
[75:55] It's all good. But uh but yeah, I think the more volume that you post, the more you give away, but it just has to be like balanced out, you know?
[76:10] Yeah. Sweet.
[76:15] All right. Very good. Give it up for Jeremy.
[76:20] Thank you.
📊 RESUMO EXECUTIVO
NÚMEROS PRINCIPAIS:
$100M+ em vendas geradas via shoutouts e conteúdo orgânico
$3.1M em vendas no último mês (8-9 clientes)
Custo por call: <$30 (calls qualificadas)
Show rate: 80-85%+ consistentemente
Cash por call: $1,389 média ($2,900 máximo registrado)
6 clientes atingindo $1M/mês consistente
13 clientes adicionando +$500K/mês
Lead-to-call ratio: 10%+ (20%+ para DM funnel ads)
$50K-$100K/mês em conversões extras de deals marginais
2,000-3,000 leads re-engajados/mês com split testing
350+ leads/dia processados por 1 setter (37 calls/dia)
Setter top: $25K/mês em comissões
Cost per follower ideal: <$0.50-$1.00
Engajamento viral: 30-50%+ em outlier posts
9 horas de conteúdo consumido overnight = 3 closes em 1 semana
Cliente ads: 2.5x ROAS → 2-3x ROAS em 3-4 semanas (mesmo budget)
ESTRATÉGIAS E SISTEMAS:
Sistema de Aquisição de Conteúdo $100M:
Conteúdo orgânico → Posts virais (30-50% engagement) → Redistribuição em theme pages → Amplificação de tráfego
Framework de 3 Componentes:
Content (80/20 principal)
DMs (alavanca de eficiência)
Shoutouts (amplificador)
Funil de Conteúdo Estratégico:
TOFU: Alcance 1M-2M+ (Reels)
MOFU: Nurturing tático (Threads/Carousels = 80% das vendas)
BOFU: CTAs e DMs (Stories para personalidade)
Processo de DM Setting:
Discovery: Gauge interesse + DQ rápido + Avaliar conhecimento
Nurture: Recursos adaptativos (24h para consumir)
Game Plan: Criar buyer intent antes da pitch
Booking: Adaptive script (não rígido)
Nurture Assets (Game Changers):
Webinar recordings (sem pitch)
Challenges 3-4 dias (9 horas de conteúdo)
Free courses YouTube (what to do, not how)
Objection handling videos específicos
Client case studies (dummy theory)
Niche-specific podcasts
Pre-call Notion asset (FAQ + testimonials + recursos)
Sistema de Setter + Closer Tag Team:
Setters com autoridade para "cozinhar" leads
Closers alertam setters sobre leads hesitantes
Colaboração fecha deals marginais
Compensação Performance-Based:
UK/US setters (não VAs baratos)
3-5% cash collected
Menos setters, melhor pagos
ESTRUTURA:
Content Schedule 5-7 dias:
FAQ tactical guide
Breakdown de outcomes
Creative objection handling
Origin story
Picture dump
Tactical threads
Stories Strategy:
2x/dia: Document day (relevante ao nicho)
1x/semana: Q&A
1-2x/semana: Results/tactical tips
Behind the scenes
Student wins 1x/semana (não diário)
Lead Generation:
Comment CTA (recurso grátis → conversão sneaky)
Direct DM CTA ("DM palavra X" → conversa direta)
Métricas de Eficiência:
Cost per follower (<$0.50-$1.00)
Lead-to-call ratio (10%+ orgânico, 20%+ ads)
Show rate (75-85%+)
Cash per call ($1,000+ benchmark, $1,389 atual)
Close rate (secundário ao cash per call)
DADOS FINANCEIROS:
ROI médio: $1,389 por call agendada
Range: $700 (problema) até $2,900 (excelente)
Conversões extras: $50K-$100K/mês de deals marginais
Cliente ads: ROAS aumentou 2-3x mantendo mesmo budget
Setter compensation: 3-5% cash collected
Setter top earning: $25K/mês
Cost per follower at scale: <$0.50
